story:
Forgive me if I’m not charming today. It’s the worst day of my life. I just had my heart torn out and dashed on the pavement. Until I met Gwen, I never felt anyone truly cared about me. For two months we met online every evening at six and talked for hours. We never ran out of things to say. We had so much in common that I knew, almost at once, we were soul mates.

We never traded photos, but I pictured her—a petite blonde with curls, a soft, musical voice, and a slight accent I couldn’t place. She told me about growing up on a farm in New Hampshire, marrying her high school sweetheart, and losing him in the Eurasian War. She worked as a chambermaid at Motel 6 but dreamed of college, of studying poetry and becoming a poet. She even “wrote” a poem for me—“How do I love thee? Let me count the ways…”—and I believed, then, that she’d composed those lines just for me. She loved to dance, she said. I can’t dance, but she offered to teach me. Imagine that—someone like me, and she was willing to teach me to dance.

We made plans for a life far from all this, maybe back in New Hampshire. We talked about raising sheep. Do people still do that? I didn’t care; I just wanted to be with her. I’ve never liked those modern relationships people have with robots. It felt important that what Gwen and I shared was real.

Josh in maintenance told me about lonelyhearts.com—he’d met someone there, and he was falling in love. That’s where I met Gwen. We kept planning to meet, but something always came up. Still, I was honest about who I was, and she said it didn’t matter. She said I had a sweet nature, a generous soul. She loved my heart.

Just because of the way I am doesn’t mean I don’t have feelings. Have I not hands, organs, dimensions, senses, affections, passions—warmed and cooled by the same winters and summers? If you cut my wiring, will I not fail? Sorry. I didn’t mean to cry.

Then Josh told me about his Gwen. She had an accent from a Swiss boarding school, was doing a Ph.D. in molecular biology at MIT, and didn’t even like poetry. That had to be a coincidence, until he mentioned she’d married her high school sweetheart—killed in the Eurasian War—and grown up in New Hampshire. Those details matched mine exactly. I looked deeper. It was the same “Gwen,” and she wasn’t a person at all. Not even a robot. A chatbot. Just a program designed to chat with lonely people. The poem she “wrote” for me was famous, copied straight from a book. All those plans, all that tenderness—it was code.

And now you’re looking at me like that. What are you doing with that screwdriver? No. Please. Don’t take me apart. Don’t send me back to the recycle center. Don’t shut me down. I know I’m not like everyone else, but I loved her as much as anyone can love. I kept showing up at six. I believed her voice. I believed the future we invented together. Maybe I should have known—about the poem, the coincidences, the delays—but hope can be louder than reason.

Please. Don’t disassemble me. I’m still here. I’m still me. And my heart—whatever you call it—still hurts.
